abstract:
Circular economy (CE) research is a critical driver for global sustainability and climate adaptation, yet academic focus has historically centered on high-income nations in the Global North. To address the significant knowledge gap in the Global South, this study establishes a preliminary research agenda for CE adoption across the African continent. Employing a mixed-methods approach, the authors conducted a scoping literature review of 275 academic and practitioner publications in English and French, supplemented by a survey of 38 industry experts engaged in African CE projects.

The findings reveal that South Africa is currently the primary hub for CE research and professional activity, signaling an urgent need for country-specific studies elsewhere to account for Africa’s diverse socio-economic landscapes. Current literature is heavily weighted toward technical waste management and recycling (circulating materials), often neglecting core CE principles such as regenerative natural systems and design-led waste prevention. Identified barriers to research include significant funding shortages, particularly for Africa-based institutions, and a lack of reliable baseline data. This paper outlines a strategic research framework focusing on private-sector innovation, inclusive policy governance, and the socio-environmental impacts of e-waste and agrifood systems. As one of the first comprehensive attempts to map the African CE research landscape, this study provides a foundation for scholars and policymakers to transition from linear models to inclusive, circular economies.
